nativeRange.habitat: The Three-Toed Amphiuma occupies a wide range of permanent and semi-permanent shallow freshwater habitats across the south-central United States, including alluvial and calcareous swamps, bottomland marshes, cypress sloughs, bayous, sluggish streams, lakes, seeps, roadside ditches, and manmade canals. It is most commonly found in heavily vegetated, mud-bottomed waters where dense cover is available, and frequently takes shelter in crayfish burrows.

decorPreferences: long footprint over depth (120 cm+) to accommodate eel-like body
soft sand or fine smooth-gravel substrate for burrowing
PVC pipe shelters or smooth caves sized to the animal's diameter
large driftwood pieces and rockwork to create hiding caves
thick layer of floating plants (duckweed, water sprite, hornwort) to diffuse light and provide cover
subdued lighting — strongly nocturnal, bright light causes chronic stress
heavy, tightly-sealed, ventilated lid (will climb or jump out of poorly secured tanks)
moderate filtration with no exposed intakes large enough to entangle the animal
moderate water depth (animal must be able to reach the surface to breathe air)

diet.notes: The Three-Toed Amphiuma is an opportunistic carnivore whose wild diet centres on crayfish — the documented preferred prey — along with earthworms, aquatic insects, snails, small fish, frogs and tadpoles, other amphibians, small reptiles, and a range of other small invertebrates. In captivity most individuals readily accept large earthworms or nightcrawlers as a staple, along with crayfish, raw or thawed prawns and shrimp, lean fish chunks (such as smelt, silverside, or tilapia fillet), and large sinking carnivore pellets. Most specimens transition from live to thawed prey with patience and time to adjust. Adults should be fed to satiation roughly once a week; juveniles benefit from roughly twice-weekly feedings. Prey should always be offered with long forceps, as bites are powerful and painful and the animal can latch on. Feeder goldfish are best avoided, as they tend to be fatty, carry a thiaminase burden (an enzyme that breaks down vitamin B1), and introduce disease risk.

avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed
shrimp, snails, and other invertebrates (will be eaten)
conspecifics (cannibalistic toward smaller individuals; aggression between adults)
other amphibians (will predate frogs, smaller salamanders)
live aquarium plants in the substrate (uprooted by burrowing)

summary: A fully aquatic, eel-like salamander from south-central US swamps and bayous, reaching about 1 m with three small toes on each tiny limb. A nocturnal ambush predator with a powerful, painful bite, it is considered suitable only for specialist keepers with a dedicated species-only setup.
careNotes:
The Three-Toed Amphiuma is one of three living members of the relict family Amphiumidae, all confined to the southeastern United States. It is the south-central counterpart to the Two-Toed Amphiuma (Amphiuma means) of the Atlantic and eastern Gulf coast and is broadly regarded as the dominant amphiuma west of the Mississippi. The two species are easily separated by digit count: three toes per limb on A. tridactylum versus two on A. means. Adult body length is typically 46–76 cm (about 18–30 in), with record specimens reaching roughly 105–117 cm (about 41–46 in). The eel-like body bears four vestigial limbs, each with three small toes, along with small functionally near-blind eyes and a wedge-shaped head with strong jaws. The species is also notable biologically for having the largest known red blood cells of any animal, measuring approximately 70 × 40 μm.

This is a strictly specialist species. Adult size, escape behaviour, dietary demands, longevity, and bite risk combine to make it a long-term commitment requiring a dedicated species-only setup. Local regulations should be checked before acquiring — some US states list amphiumas as restricted wildlife.

Tank requirements: A long, tightly lidded, fully aquatic enclosure with a minimum footprint of 120 cm and a volume of at least 500 L is generally advised for a single adult, with larger specimens benefiting from more space. Width and length matter much more than depth; the animal must be able to reach the surface to gulp air. A soft sand or smooth fine-gravel substrate is needed for burrowing, as sharp gravel can abrade the skin. Multiple PVC pipe sections or smooth-stone caves matched to the animal's body diameter should be provided as daytime shelters. A thick mat of floating plants helps diffuse light, which is important because this species is strongly nocturnal and tends to be stressed by bright illumination. The lid must be heavy, tight, and gap-free, as amphiumas have been documented climbing or jumping out of poorly secured tanks and leaving water voluntarily after heavy rain.

Water quality and parameters: Excellent water quality is essential. Although tolerant of warm Gulf-coast conditions in the wild, captives are generally advised to be kept on the cool side, with published hobbyist guidance recommending that water temperature not rise above about 26–27°C (80°F). No heater is typically required in temperate indoor rooms. pH is reported to be flexible across slightly acidic to slightly basic ranges; hardness is rarely specified in husbandry sources, but moderately soft to moderately hard freshwater appears to be tolerated. Ammonia and nitrite must be kept at zero. Like all amphibians, the Three-Toed Amphiuma has high skin sensitivity to chemical contaminants such as chlorine, copper, residual cleaning products, and medications — any new tankmates should be quarantined separately, and in-tank chemical treatments should be avoided.

Behaviour and handling: Three-Toed Amphiumas are nocturnal, secretive ambush predators that tend to spend daylight hours burrowed in substrate or coiled inside shelters, with activity peaking around midnight. Like other amphiumas, they communicate via faint underwater clicks. Handling should be minimised and carried out only with thick gloves and proper restraint. Bites can break skin and are described as painful, though rarely of medical significance beyond the mechanical injury itself. Despite the dramatic folk names — Congo Eel, Lamper Eel, Congo Snake — the species is not venomous.

Overfeeding should be avoided: adults fed more frequently than roughly once a week tend toward shortened lifespan and accelerated water-quality decline. A tightly sealed lid, moderate-to-strong filtration with guarded intakes, a generous tank footprint, soft burrowing substrate, multiple body-diameter shelters, and subdued lighting are all considered essential for the long-term wellbeing of this species.
breedingNotes: The Three-Toed Amphiuma is rarely bred in captivity. In the wild, reproduction occurs via internal fertilisation — a trait shared across the Amphiumidae family — with mating reported between December and June and nesting between April and October. Females lay a single strand of roughly 50–200 eggs in an underwater cavity or burrow, then coil around the clutch for an approximately four-to-five month incubation period; during dry spells, females have been observed aestivating (entering a dormant state to survive drought conditions) while still wrapped around the eggs. Males are polygynous and court females with elaborate spiral movements. Captive breeding has been attempted but is not routine, and is generally regarded as beyond the scope of a standard home setup. Success requires a very large enclosure offering both an aquatic section and a moist terrestrial laying area, along with simulated seasonal temperature and photoperiod cues and an increased feeding regimen to bring adults into breeding condition.

primary.name: Aqua Imports
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Used for fully-aquatic setup framing, tightly-covered tank requirement (climbing/jumping escape risk), driftwood/cave decor recommendation, sensory adaptation note (minimal vision; lateral line + scent for hunting), and trade availability/pricing context (~$80).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Three-toed amphiuma
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (Amphiumidae, Urodela), adult size record (~104.8 cm) and typical range (46–76 cm), native range (Gulf states Alabama to Texas, north to Missouri/Arkansas/Tennessee/Kentucky), habitat (bottomland marshes, lakes, seeps, bayous, cypress sloughs, crayfish burrows, ditches), breeding (December–June mating, April–October nesting, internal fertilisation, ~200-egg single-strand clutches, polygynous males), nocturnal behaviour and rain-driven terrestrial excursions, IUCN Least Concern status, and the biological note about having the largest known animal red blood cells (~70 × 40 μm).

additional.name: Caudata Culture — Amphiuma
additional.notes: Authoritative hobbyist-specialist source for caudates (covers all three Amphiuma species). Used for max size (~106 cm), upper temperature limit (~26.7°C / 80°F) and cold tolerance (well below 4.4°C, no heater needed), tank-setup guidance (long/wide over tall, secure ventilated lid, PVC pipe shelters, thick floating-plant mat, minimum water depth 10–15 cm), diet (pellets, small fish, frogs, tadpoles, crayfish, raw/cooked shrimp) and feed-to-satiation-weekly cadence, bite warning ('painful, though rarely of medical significance'), cannibalism toward smaller conspecifics, and breeding biology (elaborate spiral courtship, ~100-egg clutches in burrows, 4–5 month incubation, cloacal sexual dimorphism).

additional.name: Animal Diversity Web — Amphiuma tridactylum
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum body length (117 cm), south-central US range (south-eastern Missouri to eastern Texas, through Gulf states and into Arkansas/western Tennessee), preferred swamp/ditch/stream/lake habitats, captive lifespan range (13–19 years, with a close relative reaching 27 years), nocturnal activity peak around midnight, small home range with ability to travel ~400 m, carnivorous-generalist diet with crayfish as preferred prey, clutch size (50–200 eggs), internal fertilisation, maternal parental care including aestivation while wrapped in eggs, and IUCN Least Concern status.

amphibian.breedingNotes: Reproduction involves internal fertilisation via spermatophore transfer, with mating typically occurring between December and June and nesting between April and October. Females lay 50–200 eggs in a single strand deposited inside underwater burrows or cavities, then coil around the clutch for the four-to-five month incubation period, aestivating with the eggs during dry periods. Males are polygynous and engage in elaborate spiral courtship behaviour. Captive breeding has been attempted but is not routine, and success requires a very large enclosure offering both aquatic and moist terrestrial laying zones, along with simulation of seasonal temperature and photoperiod cycles.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: The Three-Toed Amphiuma can reach up to approximately 117 cm and requires a dedicated species-only enclosure of at least 120 cm in length and 500 litres, with a heavy escape-proof lid, powerful filtration, and a prey-based diet. It delivers a painful bite, has been documented climbing or jumping out of poorly secured tanks, is legally restricted as wildlife in some US states, and carries an expected captive lifespan of 15 to 25 or more years. It is not appropriate for a typical home aquarium setup.
